Title: Title
osysHome is a smart home system develope: osysHome is a smart home system developed in Python that offers users flexible and powerful tools to automate and manage their living space. The system is based on object-oriented programming, which ensures modularity, ease of use and ease of expansion of functionality.
welcome_intro: This smart home management system is based on object-oriented programming, ensuring modularity and ease of expansion.
welcome_step_1_title: Step 1: Explore Installed Modules and Add Integrations
welcome_step_1_description: Core modules (Objects, Users, Scheduler, etc.) are already installed. You can add additional modules for device integration, monitoring, and system management.
welcome_step_1_detail: In the "Modules" section, you'll find installed plugins and can add new ones: smart device integrations (Zigbee2MQTT, ESPHome, Xiaomi, Yandex, etc.), system monitoring (Logs, xray), backup, and more.
welcome_step_2_description: Classes define the structure and behavior of objects. Create a class, add properties and, if needed, a template for display on the control panel.
welcome_step_2_detail: Classes are templates for creating objects. They can inherit from each other and contain properties and methods.
welcome_step_2_example_class:
Class name: Switch
Description: Virtual switch
welcome_step_2_example_property:
Property: state
Type: bool
Description: Switch state
Default value: false
welcome_step_2_example_method:
Method: turnOn
Description: Turn on light (for automation and calls from other objects)
Code: setProperty(object.id, 'state', True)

Method turnOff: setProperty(object.id, 'state', False)

These methods can be called from Scheduler or from linked_method when other objects' properties change (see step 5).
welcome_step_2_example_template: {{ object.name }}
welcome_step_3_description: Objects are instances of classes with specific values. Create an object based on a class and set initial property values.
welcome_step_3_detail: Objects contain property values and can execute methods. You can create an object based on a class or as a standalone object.
welcome_step_3_example_object:
Object name: LivingRoomLight
Class: Switch
Description: Living room light
Room: Living Room
welcome_step_3_example_property_value:
Property state: false (off)

After creation, the object will inherit the template and methods (turnOn, turnOff) from the class. They can be called from Scheduler or from linked_method — see step 5 (Automation).
welcome_step_4_title: Step 4: Add Widget to Control Panel
welcome_step_4_description: Widgets display objects and modules on the control panel. Objects with templates can be added as widgets. Enable edit mode (pencil button in top right), then click "+" to add a widget.
welcome_step_4_detail: In edit mode, you can move, resize, and delete widgets. Changes are saved automatically when you exit edit mode.
welcome_step_4_example_title: Example: Adding Object to Control Panel
welcome_step_4_example_steps:
1. Ensure the class or object has a template
2. Click the "Edit" button (pencil) in top right
3. Click the "+" button (Add widget)
4. Go to the "Objects" tab
5. Select your object (e.g., LivingRoomLight)
6. The object will appear on the panel as a widget
7. Click "Save" button to exit edit mode
welcome_step_4_description_old: Widgets are displayed automatically: widgets from installed modules and objects from panel settings are shown without additional configuration.
welcome_step_4_switch_detail: On the "Customize grid" panel you can add, move, and remove widgets. Click the button below — SystemVar.control_panel_style will be set and the page will reload. You can return to the old panel anytime: in the Objects module open the SystemVar object and set control_panel_style to "Old style grid".
welcome_step_4_switch_back: To return to the old panel (without widget customization), in the Objects module change the SystemVar object's control_panel_style property to "Old style grid".
welcome_step_5_description: There are two automation methods: 1) Scheduler - execution by schedule (cron) or at specific time. 2) Event-driven - methods that execute when object properties change.
welcome_step_5_detail: Scheduler allows changing property values, calling object methods, or executing custom code on schedule.
welcome_step_5_example_scheduler:
Task: Turn on light at 6:00 PM every day
Schedule (cron): 0 18 * * *
Action: setProperty('LivingRoomLight', 'state', true)

Or call a method:
callMethod('LivingRoomLight', 'turnOn')
welcome_step_5_example_event:
Automation: Turn on light when motion detected

1. Create MotionSensor object with motion property (bool)
2. Create method onMotionChanged in MotionSensor object
3. In motion property parameters specify:
   linked_method: onMotionChanged
   (only a method of this same object!)
4. In onMotionChanged method code write:
   setProperty('LivingRoomLight', 'state', True)
   or call object method: callMethod('LivingRoomLight', 'turnOn')

When motion changes, onMotionChanged is called automatically; inside it you control LivingRoomLight.
welcome_step_5_not_installed: Scheduler module is not installed. Install it to use automation.
welcome_tip_1: The system is based on object-oriented approach — all entities are objects with properties and methods.
welcome_tip_2: All objects are stored in the database. Changes are saved automatically.
welcome_tip_3: Widgets update in real-time via WebSocket connection.
welcome_tip_4: System logs are available in the "Logs" module (if installed). Use them for troubleshooting.
welcome_tip_5: Difference between modules and objects: modules are system plugins, objects are your devices and entities.
welcome_example_1_description: Create a "Switch" class with a "state" property of type bool. This object can be used for testing automations.
welcome_example_1_steps:
1. Create 'Switch' class
2. Add 'state' property (bool)
3. Create an object, e.g., 'TestSwitch'
4. Add widget to control panel
5. Use for testing automations
welcome_example_2_description: Create a "TempSensor" class with "temperature" property (float, min=0, max=50) and "humidity" (int, 0-100). Add value validation.
welcome_example_2_steps:
1. Create 'TempSensor' class
2. Add 'temperature' property (float, min=0, max=50)
3. Add 'humidity' property (int, min=0, max=100)
4. Configure step for precision
5. Create objects for different rooms
welcome_example_3_description: Create a "SmartLight" class with properties: "power" (bool) and "brightness" (int, 0-100, step=10). Configure dependency: brightness works only when power=true.
welcome_example_3_steps:
1. Create 'SmartLight' class
2. Add 'power' property (bool)
3. Add 'brightness' property (int, 0-100, step=10)
4. In 'brightness' settings add dependency: depends_on={'property':'power', 'value':true}
5. Create objects for lights in different rooms
welcome_example_4_description: Class "Room" holds climate data for a room for automation: temperature, humidity, target temperature. Objects: living room, bedroom, etc.
welcome_example_4_steps:
1. Create class 'Room'
2. Properties: temperature (float, 0-50), humidity (int, 0-100), target_temperature (float, 5-35)
3. Optional: name (str), description (str)
4. Create objects LivingRoom, Bedroom, Kitchen
5. Use target_temperature in automation to control climate per room
welcome_example_5_description: Class "Mode" — a system mode (night, day, away). Property active (bool): when true, system is in this mode. In the method, when activated, you can control objects: turn off lights, change temperature.
welcome_example_5_steps:
1. Create class 'Mode'
2. Property active (bool), default false
3. Method onActivate: in code setProperty('LivingRoomLight', 'state', False), setProperty('BedroomLight', 'state', True), etc.
4. Manual activation: set active=true on object 'NightMode'
5. Or by Scheduler: at 23:00 setProperty('NightMode', 'active', True), then callMethod('NightMode', 'onActivate')
welcome_example_6_description: Class "Scenario" — one-button scenario: e.g. "Movie" — dim blinds, turn off main light, turn on ambient. Method run() performs multiple actions on objects.
welcome_example_6_steps:
1. Create class 'Scenario'
2. Properties: name (str), description (str)
3. Method run(): setProperty('MainLight', 'state', False), setProperty('AmbientLight', 'brightness', 20), setProperty('Blinds', 'position', 0), etc.
4. Create object 'Movie', bind to widget or button to call callMethod('Movie', 'run')
5. Can call run() from Scheduler or via linked_method when a button is pressed
welcome_example_7_description: Class "Device" — base class for devices: room link (room), power (bool), name. Other classes (lights, outlets, sensors) can inherit from it.
welcome_example_7_steps:
1. Create class 'Device'
2. Properties: name (str), room (str — Room object name or key), power (bool)
3. Optional: description (str), location (str)
4. Create class SmartLight with parent Device, add brightness
5. Create device objects from SmartLight, set room to bind to a room
welcome_example_8_description: Class "Sensor" — sensor with value property. Set linked_method on value: when value changes, that object's method is called; inside it you can read value and control other objects (turn on heater, send notification).
welcome_example_8_steps:
1. Create class 'Sensor'
2. Property value (float or int), add min/max, step if needed
3. Method onValueChanged: if object.value < 18: setProperty('Heater', 'state', True)
4. In value property parameters set linked_method: onValueChanged
5. When value is updated (manually, from module or API), onValueChanged is called automatically
